title: Battenberg cake
subtitle: Chequered pink and yellow sponge wrapped in marzipan
excerpt: Two sponge batters baked in adjacent halves of a divided tin, assembled in a chequered four-square pattern, and wrapped in a thin layer of marzipan. Makes one Battenberg, 8–10 slices.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: chequered sponge cakes with marzipan wrapping appear in the Victorian household confectionery entries. The Battenberg is named after the 1884 marriage of Prince Henry of Battenberg to Princess Beatrice, and the pattern is recorded in British household baking manuals from the 1880s onwards. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): the Battenberg documented as a standard British afternoon tea cake requiring two differently coloured sponges assembled in a chequered block.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The sponge can be baked a day ahead, wrapped, and assembled the following day.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; 190°C in a conventional oven

glossaryTerms.definition: Apricot jam warmed and sieved to remove any fruit pieces, then brushed over a cake surface as an adhesive layer before applying marzipan or fondant. The thin layer of warm jam sets as it cools and holds the marzipan firmly in place. Heat until the jam is runny but not caramelised — overheating makes it too thick to spread evenly.

content.text: A Battenberg needs two things: a divided tin (or a foil divider in a square tin) and patience when assembling. The sponge itself is a standard Victoria ratio. The pink is just a small amount of red food colouring in one half of the batter. The marzipan should be rolled thin; thick marzipan overwhelms the sponge.

Preheat the oven to 180°C fan. Line a 20 cm square tin with baking paper and make a foil divider down the centre to create two equal rectangular sections. Beat the {{unsalted-butter}} and {{caster-sugar}} until pale and fluffy. Beat in the {{eggs}} one at a time. Fold in the {{self-raising-flour}}, {{baking-powder}}, {{vanilla-extract}}, and {{whole-milk}}. Divide the batter into two equal portions. Leave one plain and colour the other pink with a small amount of red food colouring. Spoon each batter into one section of the tin and level the surfaces. Bake for 23–25 minutes, until a skewer comes out clean. Cool completely in the tin.
Trim each sponge to a neat rectangle of the same dimensions. Cut each in half lengthwise to produce four long strips: two yellow, two pink. Brush each cut surface with the
made from warmed {{apricot-jam}}. Assemble in a chequered pattern: yellow–pink on the bottom row, pink–yellow on top, pressing the strips together firmly.

On a surface dusted with icing sugar, roll the {{marzipan}} into a rectangle large enough to wrap around the assembled sponge. Brush the outside of the sponge block with apricot glaze and roll in the marzipan, pressing to adhere. Trim the ends neatly and press the seam closed at the base. Slice to serve.
